activityOfUser.occupation: Tvořím junior.guru, které pomáhá juniorním programátorům

text:
🔥 BREAKING: On the back of our "Jagged Frontier" blog post last week, we built "nano-analyzer" - a simple zero-day scanner (one Python file, one dependency, no agentic loop beyond simple grep) - and ran it on FreeBSD, OpenBSD, and OpenSSL, with some of the cheapest models available. 

This time, no hints - the models received entire source files with no guidance on where to look. 

It surfaced the same FreeBSD vulnerability (CVE-2026-4747) that Anthropic highlighted as a flagship Mythos result, multiple kernel bug candidates across FreeBSD and OpenBSD including a new bug now confirmed by the maintainer, and several additional findings under active investigation. A responsibly disclosed FreeBSD networking issue (AISLE-2026-E445) appears to have been in the codebase for over 20 years. 

Perhaps most striking: an open-weights model with just 5.1B active parameters independently surfaced the Mythos flagship vulnerability and multiple additional bug candidates - at 600x lower cost per token. 

💥 We're open-sourcing the entire analyzer codebase today.💥

Models happily provided by our friends at Hugging Face (cheers Clem Delangue 🤗, Thomas Wolf, Julien Chaumond 👋)

Excited to be building this with Stanislav Fort and Jaya Baloo

More details in the first comment.

LET'S GO AISLE™! 🚀

profile.occupation: Founder/Chief Scientist at AISLE | ex-Anthropic & DeepMind | Stanford AI/ML PhD | Forbes 30 under 30

Hi Ondrej, about Avast. Why is there no contact info on the Website according to EU GDPR dispositions? I am getting contacted, and there is no support team either. Plus, one cannot answer the bot that ignores your customer care, as if you cancel sth. via Google PlayStore, the system whines that the payment does not go through? Can you please tell me how to contact customer support or either just follow through with finishing GooglePlay Plans? I will not keep writing with a bot! And I refrain from using Avast. author.occupation: Senior Cyber Security Analyst, (ISC)² CC Certified @UVN Praha (Military University Hospital Prague), Security Enthusiast-Thinking Differently about Security, ISMS/ISO27000, DFE, EHE.

comments.text:
Still apples to bananas. A single-file scanner with grep, preloaded C/C++ bug archetypes, limited bug classes, no real stateful reasoning, and permissive triage is not measuring agentic Mythos-like capability, or even agentic Opus 4.6 capability. It is measuring how well cheap models can mine shallow, local bug patterns once the task has been reduced to file-level search.
One such pass will predictably surface some low-hanging fruit on targets like FreeBSD/OpenBSD, then you are largely done. Useful sometimes, sure, but that is not the hard part. Actual mining for hard-to-spot bugs on hardened surfaces is much more complex: cross-file reasoning, stateful logic, exploitability assessment, and reliable verification.
OpenSSL is the more meaningful target here, and even there the question is not "did it emit a CVE-shaped artifact?" but "did it find something nontrivial, high-impact, and independently verifiable?"
Raw bug count/CVE count is a bad scoreboard. Weak systems mostly find weak bugs. The real benchmark is whether you can find, verify, and prioritize the bugs that older tooling and smaller models systematically miss.

The nano-analyzer result is the key data point -- it shows the capability isn't locked to frontier compute. Once cheap open-weight models can reproduce CVE-worthy findings with one-file tooling, defenders need to assume attackers have the same leverage.

What gets interesting is when these scanners stop being a research demo and start living inside release workflows. If a 5.1B open-weights model can surface a 20-year-old networking issue plus new kernel candidates, the next question is not whether it finds things, it’s how teams decide exploitability, ownership, and ship/no-ship thresholds when the model has no source-level intuition, just pattern pressure.

author.occupation: Enterprise AI Adoption in Saudi Arabia | Sovereign AI Infrastructure | Helping Organizations Use AI Without Data Risk | Aliph Solutions

comments.text: The 600x cheaper token line is the real bomb here, not just the finding. Once full-file, no-hint analysis gets cheap enough, the bottleneck moves from “Can a model spot weird code” to “Can your security process absorb the candidate flood without drowning maintainers in maybe-bugs.” Detection is getting commoditized fast, triage discipline is about to matter a lot more.

author.occupation: Building Sovereign AI Infrastructure for Enterprise | Co-Founder & CTO, Aliph Solutions | Your employees use AI daily. Does your data stay within your walls?

comments.text: I’m curious how this tiny scanner could help new developers spot subtle bugs before they even learn debugging.
